chunks.text: Scientific inquiry begins with a question.
Why is the sky blue?
Why does metal conduct heat easily?
Within everyday doubt lies the seed of science.
A scientist first forms a hypothesis.
Then an experiment is designed to test it.
The conditions are carefully determined, and the equipment is prepared.
In the experiment
the factor that could affect the result is controlled as much as possible.
Only one factor is changed while the rest are kept constant.
This allows the researcher to distinguish whether the result was caused by that factor.
The data obtained is put through analysis and comparison with the hypothesis.
If the result supports the hypothesis, further testing follows.
If the hypothesis is denied, a new explanation is sought.
The heart of the scientific method is that any conclusion is temporary.
No matter how established a theory may be
there is always the possibility that new evidence will rewrite it.
It is this repeated process that has driven the progress of science.
From biological research to electronic engineering, each component is carefully examined
and the properties of each compound are described in an elaborate way.
Science is the work of an intelligence that never ceases to doubt.
